text.title: Speaker Diarization
text.summary: SpeakerDiarizer using pyannote 3.1: HuggingFace token setup, GPU requirement, alignment with transcript segments, and production gotchas.

rule.statement: Pre-cache the pyannote speaker-diarization-3.1 model (~1.5GB) during container build, not on the first transcription request.
rule.rationale: Downloading 1.5GB mid-request causes request timeouts in production. The model must be present before any diarization call runs.

rule.statement: Do not use pyannote diarization on CPU for production workloads. CPU inference is ~10x real-time, making a 1-hour file take 10 hours to process.
rule.rationale: pyannote requires GPU (CUDA) for acceptable performance. Without it, diarization is functionally unusable for anything beyond short clips.

example.note: Proper pyannote initialization with GPU detection.

rule.statement: Run diarization separately from transcription; align by timestamp midpoint. Do not interleave diarization into the transcription loop.
rule.rationale: Interleaving creates coupling between the two models, complicates error handling, and prevents independent retries of either step.

rule.statement: Add a boundary check when using segment midpoint for alignment: if (start + end) / 2 falls outside the speaker segment due to float rounding for sub-100ms segments, fall back to UNKNOWN rather than misassigning.
rule.rationale: Very short segments (speech artifacts) can produce midpoints that fall outside their own boundaries after floating-point arithmetic. Silent misassignment corrupts speaker labels for nearby segments.

rule.statement: Pass the actual number of speakers in the audio to pyannote (num_speakers parameter) rather than relying on auto-detection. Auto-detection over-segments mixed recordings.
rule.rationale: pyannote auto-detection creates spurious speaker IDs for background noise, laughter, or breathing in close-mic recordings. Known speaker count produces cleaner separation.
